results.title: All Fluorine-Free Lithium-Ion Batteries with High-Rate Capability
results.citation: Yiso Jeon et al. (2025) All Fluorine-Free Lithium-Ion Batteries with High-Rate Capability doi:10.1149/ma2025-02663068mtgabs
results.abstract:
With growing global awareness of environmental sustainability and the tightening of regulations on hazardous substances, particularly per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), the lithium-ion battery (LIB) industry is being pushed to seek alternative materials that eliminate fluorine-based components. PFAS, widely used in commercial battery systems due to their exceptional thermal and chemical stability, have now been identified as persistent organic pollutants due to their bioaccumulative and toxic nature. In anticipation of global regulatory actions, such as the European Chemicals Agency's move to restrict over 10,000 types of PFAS by 2026, it is becoming increasingly necessary to develop high-performance LIBs free of fluorinated compounds.
 Among the most prevalent fluorinated components in LIBs are poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) binders and lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF₆) electrolytes. While these have enabled commercial success, their limitations are increasingly evident. PVDF binders interact weakly with both aluminum current collectors and hydrophilic NCM particles, leading to electrode delamination and aluminum corrosion. Furthermore, PVDF undergoes dehydrofluorination in alkaline environments, generating hydrofluoric acid (HF), which compromises binder stability and safety. Similarly, LiPF₆ is highly thermally unstable and prone to decomposition into LiF, HF, and other reactive byproducts, which catalyze further degradation reactions. These fluorinated materials not only impair long-term electrochemical performance but also hinder battery recycling due to the formation of strong Li–F bonds and toxic pyrolysis products.
 To address these challenges, recent research has focused on fluorine-free alternatives in both binder and electrolyte systems. Aqueous binders such as polyacrylic acid (PAA), carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), and carrageenan have been studied as PVDF replacements. However, their performance is typically inadequate in high-energy Ni-rich NCM cathodes, which are highly sensitive to moisture. On the electrolyte side, fluorine-free salts such as lithium bis(oxalato)borate (LiBOB), lithium phosphate-based salts, and lithium borates have been considered. LiBOB, in particular, offers good thermal stability and aluminum passivation but suffers from poor solubility in carbonate solvents, limiting its use in high-voltage systems. To date, most studies have addressed either the binder or electrolyte in isolation, falling short of presenting a fully integrated, fluorine-free LIB system.
 In this study, we present a comprehensive approach to achieving a fully fluorine-free LIB by simultaneously replacing both the binder and electrolyte with non-fluorinated alternatives. A novel aromatic polyamide (APA) binder was synthesized, designed with polar functional groups including –CONH–, –COOH, and sulfonyl (–SO₂–) moieties. These groups enable strong hydrogen bonding interactions with the active materials and current collectors, resulting in enhanced mechanical stability and uniform electrode morphology. In addition, the amorphous nature of APA facilitates lithium-ion transport across the electrode. The binder demonstrated sevenfold higher adhesion strength compared to PVDF, as well as improved structural integrity under cycling conditions.
 For the electrolyte, we adopted a LiClO₄-based system (LC) due to its low cost, high solubility, and favorable ionic conductivity in carbonate solvents. To address the high-voltage instability of LiClO₄ alone, the electrolyte was modified with vinylene carbonate (VC) and propylene sulfone (PS) as additives, along with a small amount of LiBOB to enhance aluminum passivation. The resulting LC electrolyte exhibited approximately 85% of the ionic conductivity of conventional LiPF₆-based systems and showed oxidation stability up to ~5 V vs. Li/Li⁺, making it suitable for high-voltage cathode operation.
 Electrochemical evaluation of the full-cell systems demonstrated that the combination of APA binder and LC electrolyte maintained stable cycling performance, high coulombic efficiency, and minimal capacity fading. The performance was further validated by assembling a 1.5 Ah pouch-type cell, which retained structural integrity and electrochemical stability without any fluorinated components. This study is among the first to demonstrate a practical, scalable, and fully fluorine-free LIB configuration capable of meeting the demands of high-capacity applications.
 Overall, our work highlights the potential of an integrated fluorine-free materials approach to overcome the inherent limitations of PFAS-based LIB technologies. By replacing both PVDF and LiPF₆ with functionally optimized, non-fluorinated materials, we offer a promising pathway toward environmentally benign, high-performance, and regulation-compliant next-generation lithium-ion batteries.

results.title: All Fluorine-Free Lithium-Ion Batteries with High-Rate Capability
results.citation: Yiso Jeon et al. (2025) All Fluorine-Free Lithium-Ion Batteries with High-Rate Capability doi:10.1149/ma2025-02663068mtgabs
results.abstract:
With growing global awareness of environmental sustainability and the tightening of regulations on hazardous substances, particularly per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), the lithium-ion battery (LIB) industry is being pushed to seek alternative materials that eliminate fluorine-based components. PFAS, widely used in commercial battery systems due to their exceptional thermal and chemical stability, have now been identified as persistent organic pollutants due to their bioaccumulative and toxic nature. In anticipation of global regulatory actions, such as the European Chemicals Agency's move to restrict over 10,000 types of PFAS by 2026, it is becoming increasingly necessary to develop high-performance LIBs free of fluorinated compounds.
 Among the most prevalent fluorinated components in LIBs are poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) binders and lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF₆) electrolytes. While these have enabled commercial success, their limitations are increasingly evident. PVDF binders interact weakly with both aluminum current collectors and hydrophilic NCM particles, leading to electrode delamination and aluminum corrosion. Furthermore, PVDF undergoes dehydrofluorination in alkaline environments, generating hydrofluoric acid (HF), which compromises binder stability and safety. Similarly, LiPF₆ is highly thermally unstable and prone to decomposition into LiF, HF, and other reactive byproducts, which catalyze further degradation reactions. These fluorinated materials not only impair long-term electrochemical performance but also hinder battery recycling due to the formation of strong Li–F bonds and toxic pyrolysis products.
 To address these challenges, recent research has focused on fluorine-free alternatives in both binder and electrolyte systems. Aqueous binders such as polyacrylic acid (PAA), carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), and carrageenan have been studied as PVDF replacements. However, their performance is typically inadequate in high-energy Ni-rich NCM cathodes, which are highly sensitive to moisture. On the electrolyte side, fluorine-free salts such as lithium bis(oxalato)borate (LiBOB), lithium phosphate-based salts, and lithium borates have been considered. LiBOB, in particular, offers good thermal stability and aluminum passivation but suffers from poor solubility in carbonate solvents, limiting its use in high-voltage systems. To date, most studies have addressed either the binder or electrolyte in isolation, falling short of presenting a fully integrated, fluorine-free LIB system.
 In this study, we present a comprehensive approach to achieving a fully fluorine-free LIB by simultaneously replacing both the binder and electrolyte with non-fluorinated alternatives. A novel aromatic polyamide (APA) binder was synthesized, designed with polar functional groups including –CONH–, –COOH, and sulfonyl (–SO₂–) moieties. These groups enable strong hydrogen bonding interactions with the active materials and current collectors, resulting in enhanced mechanical stability and uniform electrode morphology. In addition, the amorphous nature of APA facilitates lithium-ion transport across the electrode. The binder demonstrated sevenfold higher adhesion strength compared to PVDF, as well as improved structural integrity under cycling conditions.
 For the electrolyte, we adopted a LiClO₄-based system (LC) due to its low cost, high solubility, and favorable ionic conductivity in carbonate solvents. To address the high-voltage instability of LiClO₄ alone, the electrolyte was modified with vinylene carbonate (VC) and propylene sulfone (PS) as additives, along with a small amount of LiBOB to enhance aluminum passivation. The resulting LC electrolyte exhibited approximately 85% of the ionic conductivity of conventional LiPF₆-based systems and showed oxidation stability up to ~5 V vs. Li/Li⁺, making it suitable for high-voltage cathode operation.
 Electrochemical evaluation of the full-cell systems demonstrated that the combination of APA binder and LC electrolyte maintained stable cycling performance, high coulombic efficiency, and minimal capacity fading. The performance was further validated by assembling a 1.5 Ah pouch-type cell, which retained structural integrity and electrochemical stability without any fluorinated components. This study is among the first to demonstrate a practical, scalable, and fully fluorine-free LIB configuration capable of meeting the demands of high-capacity applications.
 Overall, our work highlights the potential of an integrated fluorine-free materials approach to overcome the inherent limitations of PFAS-based LIB technologies. By replacing both PVDF and LiPF₆ with functionally optimized, non-fluorinated materials, we offer a promising pathway toward environmentally benign, high-performance, and regulation-compliant next-generation lithium-ion batteries.

On the electrolyte side, fluorine-free salts such as lithium bis(oxalato)borate (LiBOB), lithium phosphate-based salts, and lithium borates have been considered. These groups enable strong hydrogen bonding interactions with the active materials and current collectors, resulting in enhanced mechanical stability and uniform electrode morphology. In addition, the amorphous nature of APA facilitates lithium-ion transport across the electrode. The binder demonstrated sevenfold higher adhesion strength compared to PVDF, as well as improved structural integrity under cycling conditions.
 For the electrolyte, we adopted a LiClO₄-based system (LC) due to its low cost, high solubility, and favorable ionic conductivity in carbonate solvents. To address the high-voltage instability of LiClO₄ alone, the electrolyte was modified with vinylene carbonate (VC) and propylene sulfone (PS) as additives, along with a small amount of LiBOB to enhance aluminum passivation. This study is among the first to demonstrate a practical, scalable, and fully fluorine-free LIB configuration capable of meeting the demands of high-capacity applications.

results.title: All Fluorine-Free Lithium-Ion Batteries with High-Rate Capability
results.citation: Yiso Jeon et al. (2025) All Fluorine-Free Lithium-Ion Batteries with High-Rate Capability doi:10.1149/ma2025-02663068mtgabs
results.abstract:
With growing global awareness of environmental sustainability and the tightening of regulations on hazardous substances, particularly per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), the lithium-ion battery (LIB) industry is being pushed to seek alternative materials that eliminate fluorine-based components. PFAS, widely used in commercial battery systems due to their exceptional thermal and chemical stability, have now been identified as persistent organic pollutants due to their bioaccumulative and toxic nature. In anticipation of global regulatory actions, such as the European Chemicals Agency's move to restrict over 10,000 types of PFAS by 2026, it is becoming increasingly necessary to develop high-performance LIBs free of fluorinated compounds.
 Among the most prevalent fluorinated components in LIBs are poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) binders and lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF₆) electrolytes. While these have enabled commercial success, their limitations are increasingly evident. PVDF binders interact weakly with both aluminum current collectors and hydrophilic NCM particles, leading to electrode delamination and aluminum corrosion. Furthermore, PVDF undergoes dehydrofluorination in alkaline environments, generating hydrofluoric acid (HF), which compromises binder stability and safety. Similarly, LiPF₆ is highly thermally unstable and prone to decomposition into LiF, HF, and other reactive byproducts, which catalyze further degradation reactions. These fluorinated materials not only impair long-term electrochemical performance but also hinder battery recycling due to the formation of strong Li–F bonds and toxic pyrolysis products.
 To address these challenges, recent research has focused on fluorine-free alternatives in both binder and electrolyte systems. Aqueous binders such as polyacrylic acid (PAA), carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), and carrageenan have been studied as PVDF replacements. However, their performance is typically inadequate in high-energy Ni-rich NCM cathodes, which are highly sensitive to moisture. On the electrolyte side, fluorine-free salts such as lithium bis(oxalato)borate (LiBOB), lithium phosphate-based salts, and lithium borates have been considered. LiBOB, in particular, offers good thermal stability and aluminum passivation but suffers from poor solubility in carbonate solvents, limiting its use in high-voltage systems. To date, most studies have addressed either the binder or electrolyte in isolation, falling short of presenting a fully integrated, fluorine-free LIB system.
 In this study, we present a comprehensive approach to achieving a fully fluorine-free LIB by simultaneously replacing both the binder and electrolyte with non-fluorinated alternatives. A novel aromatic polyamide (APA) binder was synthesized, designed with polar functional groups including –CONH–, –COOH, and sulfonyl (–SO₂–) moieties. These groups enable strong hydrogen bonding interactions with the active materials and current collectors, resulting in enhanced mechanical stability and uniform electrode morphology. In addition, the amorphous nature of APA facilitates lithium-ion transport across the electrode. The binder demonstrated sevenfold higher adhesion strength compared to PVDF, as well as improved structural integrity under cycling conditions.
 For the electrolyte, we adopted a LiClO₄-based system (LC) due to its low cost, high solubility, and favorable ionic conductivity in carbonate solvents. To address the high-voltage instability of LiClO₄ alone, the electrolyte was modified with vinylene carbonate (VC) and propylene sulfone (PS) as additives, along with a small amount of LiBOB to enhance aluminum passivation. The resulting LC electrolyte exhibited approximately 85% of the ionic conductivity of conventional LiPF₆-based systems and showed oxidation stability up to ~5 V vs. Li/Li⁺, making it suitable for high-voltage cathode operation.
 Electrochemical evaluation of the full-cell systems demonstrated that the combination of APA binder and LC electrolyte maintained stable cycling performance, high coulombic efficiency, and minimal capacity fading. The performance was further validated by assembling a 1.5 Ah pouch-type cell, which retained structural integrity and electrochemical stability without any fluorinated components. This study is among the first to demonstrate a practical, scalable, and fully fluorine-free LIB configuration capable of meeting the demands of high-capacity applications.
 Overall, our work highlights the potential of an integrated fluorine-free materials approach to overcome the inherent limitations of PFAS-based LIB technologies. By replacing both PVDF and LiPF₆ with functionally optimized, non-fluorinated materials, we offer a promising pathway toward environmentally benign, high-performance, and regulation-compliant next-generation lithium-ion batteries.

The binder demonstrated sevenfold higher adhesion strength compared to PVDF, as well as improved structural integrity under cycling conditions.
 For the electrolyte, we adopted a LiClO₄-based system (LC) due to its low cost, high solubility, and favorable ionic conductivity in carbonate solvents. To address the high-voltage instability of LiClO₄ alone, the electrolyte was modified with vinylene carbonate (VC) and propylene sulfone (PS) as additives, along with a small amount of LiBOB to enhance aluminum passivation. The resulting LC electrolyte exhibited approximately 85% of the ionic conductivity of conventional LiPF₆-based systems and showed oxidation stability up to ~5 V vs. Li/Li⁺, making it suitable for high-voltage cathode operation.
 Electrochemical evaluation of the full-cell systems demonstrated that the combination of APA binder and LC electrolyte maintained stable cycling performance, high coulombic efficiency, and minimal capacity fading. The performance was further validated by assembling a 1.5 Ah pouch-type cell, which retained structural integrity and electrochemical stability without any fluorinated components. This study is among the first to demonstrate a practical, scalable, and fully fluorine-free LIB configuration capable of meeting the demands of high-capacity applications.
 Overall, our work highlights the potential of an integrated fluorine-free materials approach to overcome the inherent limitations of PFAS-based LIB technologies. By replacing both PVDF and LiPF₆ with functionally optimized, non-fluorinated materials, we offer a promising pathway toward environmentally benign, high-performance, and regulation-compliant next-generation lithium-ion batteries.
